other_observers: Nick A. Komar observed bird #1.; Bird #1 was referred to us by Steve Messick, but original finder is unknown (maybe Steve himself). Bird #2 was myself. Bird #3 was myself, but was at a location where Steve Messick had seen a candidate GCTH.
duration: 20 min and 10 min and 5 min respectively
specific_location: Crow Valley Campground. Bird #1 was in the creek bed at the northwest corner, and also frequented the water drip at the picnic shelter there; Bird #2 was 200 feet south by southwest of the main Picnic pavion, along the creek; Bird #3 was 300 feet southeast of the main picnic pavilion.
description: American Robin-like passerine birds; about 25% smaller than robins; typical size and shape of Catharus species thrush.  Upperparts (tail, rump, wings, back, nape) olive-gray-brown. A browner crown was observed in one bird (see photos). Dark eye with pale gray orbital. Dark gray auriculars. White throat with black malar stripes/spotting, more spotted toward upper chest. Upper chest dotted with distinct black spots, about 1-2 mm in diameter, becoming gray on the lower chest. Chest background color white, extending to belly and vent. Flanks gray, same color as gray spots on lower chest.  Legs pale. Bill dark except for basal half of lower mandible, which was yellowish. Bill typical length of Catharus thrushes.  Underwing with pale buff stripe noticeable in flight.   The degree of spotting on the breast, heaviness of the malar stripe and brightness of the yellow base of the lower mandible was variable among the individuals observed.Behaviors: Foraging on ground, perched in trees usually within 15 feet of the ground.Call: nonePlumage: adult
photographer: Photographer: NKomar, N Komar, N Komar, N Komar, N Komar
